narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and a Year 1 school decile ranking of 8—both slightly above average—you brought some promising academic strengths to your law school application. However, your LSAT score of 21, which is notably below the typical 36.7 average, stood out as the single biggest factor pushing the model's prediction toward expecting you to fail the bar exam on your first try. The model estimates a 52% probability that you would fail, which is just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to pass with high confidence. Among all the elements considered, like your lower-middle family income bracket, full-time student status, and Year 3 decile of 5 around average, your LSAT score had the strongest influence, significantly raising the risk of bar failure in the model's eyes. Your undergraduate GPA played a helpful counterbalancing role, pulling the prediction toward success by showing consistent performance close to the 3.21 average. Similarly, your strong Year 1 decile ranking provided another positive nudge, suggesting early potential that tempered the overall risk somewhat. Compared to typical students in the dataset—who often have higher LSATs balancing out average GPAs and deciles—your profile tipped into higher failure risk primarily because that low LSAT outweighed your other solid showings. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a law student or person, but a data-driven call based on patterns from past bar takers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to average, as that could dramatically shift future predictions in your favor. Strengthening your Year 3 performance or gathering letters highlighting your resilience could also help. Many applicants in similar situations succeed by addressing their weakest area head-on—reach out to admissions advisors for targeted advice, and keep pursuing your goals with that determination you've already shown in your early academics.
